title: Coexistence of High Temperature Superconductivity and Antiferromagnetic Order in a Cuprate with Multiple Hole Fermi Pockets
authors: Xiangyu Luo, Yinghao Li, Hao Chen, Yiwen Chen, Jumin Shi, Taimin Miao, Bo Liang, Wenpei Zhu, Neng Cai, Xiaolin Ren, Yingjie Shu, Chaohui Yin, Jiuxiang Zhang, Chengtian Lin, Shenjin Zhang, Zhimin Wang, Fengfeng Zhang, Feng Yang, Qinjun Peng, Zuyan Xu, Guodong Liu, Xintong Li, Hanqing Mao, Tao Xiang, Lin Zhao, X. J. Zhou
abstract: The intricate relationship between high temperature superconductivity and antiferromagnetic order in cuprates, and the fundamental origin of electron pairing remain open questions. By utilizing high-resolution laser-based spatially-resolved angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy, we investigate the seven-layer $Bi_{2}Sr_{2}Ca_{6}Cu_{7}O_{18+δ}$ (Bi2267) and identify a cuprate system that consists of multiple hole Fermi pockets. The observed Fermi pockets exhibit pronounced momentum-, temperature- and Fermi surface-dependent energy gaps. Crucially, high temperature superconductivity with a critical temperature ($T_{\mathrm{c}}$) of $\sim$75 K emerges in a system with multiple Fermi pockets and the presence of strong antiferromagnetic order and correlations. In particular, substantial electron pairing is observed along the Fermi pocket with an energy gap up to $\sim$42 meV in lightly-doped CuO$_{2}$ planes ($p\sim$0.05). These findings challenge the conventional understanding of the roles of the nodal and antinodal electronic states in driving high-temperature superconductivity. They show that superconductivity and antiferromagnetism can coexist in a cuprate with multiple Fermi pockets, offering further insights into the pairing mechanism in cuprate superconductors.
summary: This work investigates high-temperature superconductivity in layered cuprates using ARPES, revealing the existence of multiple hole Fermi pockets coexisting with strong antiferromagnetism. By combining experimental data with a mean-field t-U model, the authors argue that robust pairing can occur even when AFM order is present, offering new insights into unconventional pairing mechanisms.
main_problem: The fundamental mechanism behind high-temperature superconductivity (HTSC) in cuprates, particularly understanding how HTSC coexists with strong antiferromagnetic (AFM) order.
main_result: Superconductivity ($T_c \sim 75	ext{ K}$) is observed in a system exhibiting multiple hole Fermi pockets that coexist robustly with strong AFM correlations. This challenges conventional views requiring the suppression of AFM order for superconductivity.
method: The study combines high-resolution Angle-Resolved Photoemission Spectroscopy (ARPES) experiments with theoretical modeling using the mean-field t-U model to analyze electronic structure and gap formation.
model_or_system: The system is a seven-layer cuprate superconductor ($	ext{Bi}_2	ext{Sr}_2	ext{CaCu}_2	ext{O}_{8+\delta}$), which exhibits multiple hole Fermi pockets derived from different $	ext{CuO}_2$ planes. The theoretical framework employs the mean-field t-U model to describe electronic correlations and interlayer coupling.
key_observables: Multiple, distinct hole Fermi pockets ($\alpha,  eta, \gamma, \delta$), momentum-, temperature-, and doping-dependent superconducting energy gaps (up to $\sim 42	ext{ meV}$), and the coexistence of $T_c$ with strong AFM order.
important_parameters: Critical temperatures ($T_c \approx 75	ext{ K}$), pairing gap magnitudes ($\Delta$), doping levels ($p \sim 0.05$), and hopping/coupling parameters derived from fitting (e.g., $t, t', t''$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the multiple Fermi pockets arise due to varying doping across different $	ext{CuO}_2$ planes within the layered structure, and that the observed gap opening in inner planes is intrinsic.
figures_summary: Figures detail ARPES measurements showing Fermi surface mappings with four distinct pocket types ($\alpha-\delta$) and energy gaps. Theoretical figures illustrate band structures and fitting parameters derived from the mean-field t-U model applied to the seven-layer structure.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first identifying the complex electronic structure via ARPES on multiple pockets, then using a theoretical framework (t-U model) to fit these experimental observations, ultimately arguing that superconductivity and AFM order can coexist in this multi-pocket regime.
why_interesting: For AMO/open quantum systems theorists, the strong correlation physics described by the Hubbard model variant (t-U) is highly relevant. The interplay between magnetic order (AFM) and pairing symmetry ($d$-wave gap structure) provides a rich platform for studying emergent collective phenomena in strongly interacting electron systems.
